sections.text: Welcome back to AP Psychology. Module Seven — memory. This is the unit that explains why you can sing every word of a song from third grade but forgot what you ate for lunch yesterday. By the end of today you'll know how memory actually works, why it fails, and the studying strategies that beat re-reading the textbook every single time. Let's go.

Quick test. You sit down to study with your textbook open and Spotify on. You read the chapter twice. You highlight a bunch of stuff. You feel productive. Then the test rolls around and you blank. What happened? You weren't actually encoding anything. Your eyes moved across the words but your brain processed them at the shallowest level — visual shape only. No meaning. No connections. No memory. That's the levels-of-processing trap, and it's why most studying is wasted effort. Today we fix that.

Three things today. One — the multi-store model: how information moves from your senses to short-term memory to long-term memory. Two — why you forget, including the AP exam's favorite trap: proactive versus retroactive interference. Three — the studying strategies that actually work, backed by research. Let's get into it.

The Atkinson-Shiffrin multi-store model, nineteen sixty-eight. Memory has three stages, like a factory assembly line. Stage one — sensory memory. Everything your eyes and ears pick up dumps in here for less than a second. Iconic memory — visual — lasts under one second. Echoic memory — auditory — lasts about three seconds. Almost all of it disappears. Only what you pay attention to moves on. Stage two — short-term memory. Capacity about seven items, plus or minus two — that's Miller's magic number. Duration about thirty seconds without rehearsal. This is your mental scratchpad. Stage three — long-term memory. Effectively unlimited capacity. Effectively unlimited duration. The information that makes it here can stick around for decades. Sensory, short-term, long-term. Three stages. Memorize.

Baddeley updated short-term memory into something richer — working memory. It has four parts. The phonological loop holds verbal and auditory information — the voice in your head when you repeat a phone number. The visuospatial sketchpad holds visual and spatial information — picturing the layout of your bedroom. The central executive is the boss — it directs attention and switches between tasks. The episodic buffer ties it all together with information from long-term memory. Working memory isn't just storage — it's the workspace where thinking happens. When you do mental math or follow a complicated sentence, that's working memory grinding.

Now the most useful idea in this module — levels of processing, Craik and Lockhart, nineteen seventy-two. How deeply you process information determines how well you remember it. Shallow processing means visual or structural — what does the word look like, is it in capital letters. You will forget it almost immediately. Phonemic — what does it sound like, what does it rhyme with. Slightly better, still weak. Deep processing means semantic — what does the word MEAN, how does it connect to things I already know. This is the level that builds durable memory. The self-reference effect is the deepest version — connecting information to yourself. If you tie a vocabulary word to your own life, you'll remember it. If you just stare at the page, you won't. Re-reading is shallow. Explaining the idea to someone is deep. That's the whole game.

Long-term memory isn't one bucket. It splits into two big branches. Explicit memory — also called declarative — is the stuff you can consciously recall and put into words. It splits again. Episodic memory is your personal experiences — your tenth birthday, your first day of high school. Semantic memory is general facts — the capital of France, what photosynthesis is. Implicit memory is the stuff you can't easily put into words. Procedural memory is skills — riding a bike, tying your shoes. You can do it but you can't explain it. Classically conditioned responses — like flinching at a loud noise — also live here. Different brain regions handle different types. The hippocampus builds explicit memories. The cerebellum and basal ganglia handle procedural ones. That's why patients with hippocampal damage can still learn new skills — they just can't remember learning them.

Your turn. You're trying to remember the new four-digit passcode for your phone, but you keep typing your old one by accident. The old code keeps blocking the new one. Question one — is this proactive or retroactive interference? Question two — what's the difference between those two? Pause. Decide. Press play.

Welcome back. The answer is PROACTIVE interference. Here's the rule. Proactive — old information blocks new information. Pro means forward — the OLD memory is moving forward in time and getting in the way of the NEW one. Retroactive — new information blocks old. Retro means backward — the NEW memory is reaching backward and overwriting the OLD one. Your old passcode interfering with the new one — old blocking new — proactive. If you learned Spanish first and now French keeps getting tangled with your Spanish — old blocking new — proactive. If you learned French first and now Spanish has overwritten the French — new blocking old — retroactive. Memorize the direction. AP loves this question.

Why do we forget? Five reasons. One — encoding failure. The information never made it into memory in the first place. Most everyday forgetting is this. You weren't paying attention, so nothing got encoded. Two — decay. Memories fade over time if not used. Most relevant for sensory and short-term memory. Three — interference. Proactive and retroactive — we just covered. Four — retrieval failure. The memory IS there, but you can't access it right now. The classic example is the tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon — you know you know it, you can almost taste it, but it won't come out. Five — motivated forgetting. Freud's idea — we push painful memories out of awareness. Less supported by modern research, but AP still tests it. Encoding failure, decay, interference, retrieval failure, motivated forgetting.

Memory isn't a video recording. It's a reconstruction. Every time you remember something, you rebuild it from fragments — and the rebuild can be wrong. Elizabeth Loftus's misinformation effect, nineteen seventy-four. She showed people a video of a car accident, then asked questions worded slightly differently. People asked how fast the cars were going when they SMASHED into each other estimated higher speeds, and were more likely to falsely remember broken glass that wasn't actually there. The wording of the question literally rewrote their memory. Source amnesia — you remember the fact but forget where you heard it, so you can confuse a dream with a real event. False memories — entirely fabricated memories that feel completely real. And schemas — your mental templates — distort recall by filling in gaps with what should have been there. Eyewitness testimony is way less reliable than juries believe.

Now the good news — strategies that actually work. Five of them. One — chunking. Group items into meaningful units. A nine-digit number is hard. Three groups of three is easy. That's how phone numbers exist. Two — mnemonics. ROY G BIV for the colors of the rainbow. Method of loci — placing items in imagined locations. Mnemonics force deep processing. Three — the spacing effect. Studying spread out over days beats cramming the night before, every single time. Your brain consolidates between sessions. Four — the testing effect — also called retrieval practice. Quizzing yourself is more effective than re-reading. Pulling information OUT strengthens the memory more than putting it back IN. This is why flashcards beat highlighting. Five — sleep. Memory consolidation happens overnight, especially during deep sleep and REM. Pulling an all-nighter to study is biochemically a terrible plan. Chunk, mnemonic, space, test, sleep. That's the studying playbook.

Recap. Six things. One — three stages: sensory under a second, short-term about thirty seconds and seven items, long-term effectively unlimited. Two — working memory has a phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, central executive, and episodic buffer. Three — deep semantic processing beats shallow visual processing. Self-reference is the deepest. Four — long-term memory splits into explicit episodic and semantic, and implicit procedural and conditioned. Five — proactive interference is OLD blocking NEW. Retroactive is NEW blocking OLD. Six — to study smart: chunk, mnemonic, space, test, sleep. Re-reading is the trap.

Important — this video is the overview. To actually master Module Seven, here's the path. One — read Myers Psychology for AP, Module Seven on memory. Especially the encoding-storage-retrieval framework and the Loftus misinformation studies. Two — go to AP Classroom and do fifteen multiple-choice questions on identifying types of memory and types of forgetting. Three — do the assignment in your dashboard: take three vocabulary words from this module and study them using the testing effect plus spacing — then take three different words and study them using re-reading only. Compare your scores in two days. Four — book a check-in with your advisor if proactive versus retroactive still feels fuzzy. It trips up everyone. See you in Module Eight — cognition and language, where we figure out how the brain solves problems and why your phone autocorrect is technically a cognitive psychology experiment.
